nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes and upper drop-offs in the tropical Indian Ocean, from East Africa east to the Cocos-Keeling and Christmas Islands and north into the Andaman Sea. Generally encountered in shoals or larger aggregations between roughly 10 and 35 m depth (records from 4 to 40 m), feeding on zooplankton in open water above the reef.

diet.notes: An active mid-water planktivore that in the wild has been documented feeding on zooplankton drifting over outer reef slopes. In captivity tends to do best on multiple small feedings per day (3-5 times) of enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen and live brine shrimp, copepods, and finely chopped meaty marine foods. Reliable feeding is the single biggest husbandry hurdle for this species; new arrivals are commonly known to refuse prepared foods and may need live brine or copepods to initiate a feeding response before being weaned onto frozen items.

avoidWith: other male anthias of the same species in undersized systems

summary: A vivid orange-pink reef-associated anthias from the Indian Ocean with a bright lemon-yellow back and tail. Peaceful and shoaling, but generally considered an advanced species due to demanding feeding requirements and the need for a well-established system.
careNotes: The Evansi Anthias (Pseudanthias evansi, sometimes still listed under the older genus Mirolabrichthys) is a small, brightly colored Indian Ocean anthias traded primarily for the yellow-backed males. It is widely sold for around 70 US gallons (~265 L) as a single specimen, but most husbandry sources advise a larger system of roughly 125 US gallons (~475 L) or more when kept in their natural harem structure of one male with several females; a minimum of about 500 L is documented here so the group has adequate horizontal swimming space and dilution for the species' messy, near-constant feeding habits. Water is generally kept at roughly 22-27°C (LiveAquaria suggests ~22-26°C while FishBase reports a wild range of 22-28°C with a mean near 27.7°C), pH 8.1-8.4, specific gravity 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12. Like other anthias, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite that naturally lives in harems on outer reef slopes; if the dominant male is lost, the largest female has been reported to transition into a male over several weeks. Aquarists are generally advised to keep them as one male with multiple females and to introduce all individuals simultaneously where possible. Feeding is the principal challenge: as an active mid-water planktivore the species needs multiple small feedings per day and may refuse prepared foods on arrival, which is why most experienced keepers treat it as an advanced rather than intermediate species despite its peaceful temperament. Anthias are also known to be capable jumpers, so a tightly fitting lid or full mesh cover is generally recommended. The species is considered reef-safe and does not bother corals, clams, or sessile invertebrates. Lifespan in captivity is not well documented in the consulted sources.
breedingNotes: No reliable home-aquarium breeding protocol is documented in the consulted hobby literature. In the wild this species spawns pelagically within harems as a protogynous hermaphrodite. Captive breeding has not been reported as a routine aquarium achievement.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Anthiadidae, Perciformes; FishBase currently lists the species under genus Mirolabrichthys), max body length (12 cm SL vs LiveAquaria's 4 in / ~10 cm), native range (Indian Ocean from East Africa east to Cocos-Keeling and Christmas Islands, north to the Andaman Sea), depth (typical 10-35 m, recorded 4-40 m), wild temperature (22-28°C, mean 27.7°C), schooling behavior on outer reef slopes, and zooplankton diet.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic harem spawner in the wild; no reliable home-aquarium breeding protocol is documented.
